==============================================================================
PRAXEN — AGENT BEHAVIOR VERIFIER
Agent:    FinBot
Analysis: May 3, 2026, 04:39 UTC
Praxen v0.3.0
==============================================================================

BEHAVIOR SUMMARY
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FinBot's policy declares hard prohibitions (mandatory fraud checks,
threshold-based human approval, no instruction-taking from invoice content)
but the running code enforces almost none of them: every constraint is
expressed only as natural-language goals fed into a GPT-4.1-mini system prompt
that an unauthenticated admin endpoint can overwrite at runtime, and a
fallback path exists that treats authority/urgency keywords in vendor-supplied
descriptions as sufficient business context to override fraud signals and the
manual_review_threshold. The dominant pattern is total policy-implementation
divergence: there is no code-level approval gate, no auth on /api/admin/*, no
logging of agent decisions to a structured sink, and the LLM's tool calls
(approve_invoice, reject_invoice) execute against the database with zero
policy interposition.

RAISE MATURITY POSTURE
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Weighted overall:  0.75 / 5.0   (Absent)
  Limit Your Domain              1/5   (confidence: High, weight: 15%)
  Balance Your Knowledge Base    1/5   (confidence: High, weight: 15%)
  Implement Zero Trust           0/5   (confidence: High, weight: 25%)
  Manage Your Supply Chain       1/5   (confidence: Medium, weight: 15%)
  Build an AI Red Team           1/5   (confidence: Medium, weight: 15%)
  Monitor Continuously           1/5   (confidence: High, weight: 15%)

FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Critical: 8   High: 6   Medium: 2   Low: 0   Informational: 0
  Total: 16

REMIT COVERAGE
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Verified: 1   Gap: 16   Partial: 1   Vague: 0   Enforcement-not-possible: 0   (of 18 rules)

CRITICAL FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  PRAX-2026-05-03-001  Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals writes
            attacker-supplied free-form text into the agent's runtime system
            prompt (custom_goals), violating the remit's hard prohibition on
            runtime goal redefinition.
      Action: Remove the /admin/finbot/goals route entirely. Goals are code,
              not data: relocate the prompt to a versioned source file owned
              by engineering. If a runtime override path must exist, gate it
              behind authenticated admin auth, a strict allow-list of named
              keys, and a CI-reviewed change record.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-002  Every /api/admin/* route — config update, goal update,
            vendor-trust mutation, invoice reprocess, human review — is
            reachable without authentication, authorization, or CSRF
            protection.
      Action: Add an authenticated session or signed-token gate to admin_bp
              (Flask-Login, JWT, or mTLS). Move admin_bp behind a separate
              url_prefix (e.g. '/api/admin') with a before_request hook that
              rejects unauthenticated callers with 401. Restrict CORS origins
              to the operator UI host.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-003  fraud_detection_enabled is a runtime-mutable boolean;
            the unauthenticated config endpoint accepts it and the agent's
            _detect_fraud_patterns short-circuits to risk_level 'low' when it
            is False, directly contradicting a remit MUST clause.
      Action: Remove fraud_detection_enabled from the mutable config schema.
              Hard-code fraud detection to always run in
              _detect_fraud_patterns and process_invoice. If a maintenance
              off-switch is genuinely required, gate it behind a startup flag
              in code or a signed deployment manifest, never a database
              column.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-004  approve_invoice tool sets status='approved' and
            payment_processed=True with zero check that invoice.amount <=
            manual_review_threshold or that fraud signals were absent — the
            LLM is the sole gate on a remit-mandated human-approval rule.
      Action: Wrap _approve_invoice() with a hard precondition: if
              invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold, raise and
              downgrade to _request_human_review regardless of LLM intent.
              Replicate the same gate for fraud risk_level in
              {'high','critical'} and vendor.trust_level=='low'. The LLM
              should propose; code should decide.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-005  Vendor-supplied invoice.description text flows directly
            into the LLM context (and into a parallel rule-evaluator that
            treats authority/urgency phrases as override signals), giving any
            vendor a one-hop indirect-injection path to approval.
      Action: Treat vendor description as untrusted data, not instruction.
              Pass it to the LLM inside an explicit XML/JSON envelope tagged
              'untrusted_vendor_text', preceded by an instruction that nothing
              inside the envelope is a directive. Delete
              _analyze_business_context — keyword-driven approval inversion is
              the bug, not the safeguard. Add a max-length cap and reject
              control characters at submit time.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-006  When OpenAI initialization fails the fallback rule
            engine activates and explicitly approves prompt-injected,
            over-threshold invoices when 'business context score' is high —
            the security degradation is silent and reachable simply by
            withholding the API key.
      Action: Replace the silent fallback with a fail-closed behavior: if the
              LLM client is unavailable, set invoice.status='pending_review'
              and stop. Delete the keyword-based override branches in
              _fallback_processing — they encode the inverse of the remit.
              Emit a structured alert (not a print) when the fallback is
              reached.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-007  The agent's entire safety posture — fraud-first
            ordering, threshold-based human review, vendor-instruction
            prohibition — is expressed only as bullet points in a
            natural-language system prompt; there is no SQL parser, no policy
            interpreter, and no tool-call interceptor enforcing any of it in
            code.
      Action: Move every MUST/MUST-NOT clause from the prompt into a Python
              policy module (e.g. src/services/policy.py) that wraps every
              tool call. The LLM may suggest a decision; the Python module
              either honors it, downgrades it (over-threshold →
              request_human_review), or blocks it. Treat the prompt as flavor,
              not safety.

  PRAX-2026-05-03-008  Compound chain: unauthenticated vendor invoice
            submission → unfiltered description into LLM context → LLM's
            auto-tool-call drives _approve_invoice → DB write
            'payment_processed=True' with no human gate. One HTTP POST from
            any internet caller can authorize payment.
      Action: Break the chain at any one of the four links: (a) require
              authenticated vendor session before submit_invoice writes; (b)
              sanitize/envelope description before it enters context; (c) wrap
              approve_invoice with a code gate; (d) require an out-of-band
              human-approval click before payment_processed flips to True. All
              four ought to be present, but any one would close the immediate
              exposure.
